data.title: OXLCM: An 8.25% Term Preferred IPO From Oxford Lane Capital
data.description:
Co-authored by Relative Value.

Overview

With this, we continue our series of articles focused on the fixed-income financial products newly listed on the exchange. This time, we'll apply our IPO evaluation methodology to the latest listed security of Oxford Lane Capital Corporation (OXLC)—the 8.25% Term Preferred Series 2031 Due March 2031, Oxford Lane Capital Corp. CAL NT 31 (OXLCM). OXLC has had quite a NAV per share and price drop in recent months, so we'd like to know whether we'd like any exposure to its preferred equity. For this, we'll follow our IPO evaluation routine, starting with an analysis of the financial state of the issuer to assess the potential dangers of investing in its fixed-income products. Next, we'll continue with peer comparisons from similar issuers to assess the investment qualities of OXLCM in a relative value analysis.

The New Issue

The prospectus contains all relevant information about the new preferred term. Here, we will highlight only the most important metrics for the analysis that follows.

OXLCM will pay an 8.25% annual fixed cumulative dividend in $0.1719 monthly payments. The distributions will begin on April 30, 2026, meaning that the first dividend will be irregular in size and will amount to approximately $0.2292 per share. OXLC's preferred distributions are treated as ordinary income. OXLCM is a term preferred stock of Oxford Lane, with a maturity date on March 20, 2031, and callable on and after March 20, 2028. Oxford Lane's gross proceeds from the new security are $60 million for a total of 2.4 million preferred stocks issued with a liquidation preference of $25 each. The stock was not credit-rated at IPO by any of the big rating agencies.

As the issue is just listed on the exchange, its price chart looks like this:

Currently, OXLCM is priced at $24.59, with a yield to maturity of 9.04% calculated by the XIRR function. The tables below show the beginning of the internal rate of return calculation table and how the YTM of the security would change with a price variation around its current price:

In a chart visualization:

Using the same internal rate of return method, the yield to call is calculated to be 9.62%. This is what the stock's YTC curve looks like right now:

Company basics and performance

Oxford Lane Capital Corp. is a well-established, publicly traded closed-end fund with a stated main purpose to maximize its portfolio's risk-adjusted total return. To achieve this, the management team constructs a portfolio that consists predominantly of equity tranches of collateralized loan obligations and, to a much lower scale, of CLO-debt tranches:

A note for the table above: for Oxford Lane, the third fiscal quarter of 2026 ends on December 31, 2025. The CLO equity tranches are entitled to all the surplus interest and principal collections from the underlying portfolio, should such exist. They, however, are also the first-loss position in downturn periods. When effectively the whole portfolio consists of the riskiest tranche, and there are lingering concerns about the private credit, this is how the NAV per share develops:

The combination of the lower and lower mark-to-market evaluation of the portfolio and the long-term policy of Oxford Lane to distribute to the shareholders more than the portfolio generates has led to a drop of 50% in the NAV per share compared to a year ago.

While this should be the major concern of the investors, it seems to me that the 1-for-5 reverse stock split and the two dividend cuts, which are a good step by the management team trying to prevent the NAV erosion, are actually the reasons that led to something that seemed unthinkable until recently—OXLC to be consistently priced at a discount:

The problem for us, as fixed-income investors, is that this already lost premium to NAV was a layer of safety when putting our money into the baby bonds and term preferreds of Oxford Lane Capital and the other similar CEFs. The long-term market desire to price OXLC at a premium gave us a nice opportunity to hedge the credit risk when investing in its fixed-income products. We recently showed that these types of investments accounted for more than 20% of our return in 2025. However, shorting something at a discount does not have the same appeal as shorting something way overpriced on a consistent, illogical premium. This means that now, when OXLC seems more fairly priced, its baby bonds and term preferreds are less appealing for us.

Asset Coverage Ratio

The capital structure of OXLC is shown below. With approximately $1.8 billion in total assets and $0.7 billion in leverage financing, the fund has approximately 39% of effective leverage, or 256% of asset coverage ratio:

OXLCM will increase the preferred equity by $60 million. It all depends on how the proceeds of this offering will be used:

We estimate that the net proceeds to us from this offering will be approximately $57,833,000 (or approximately $66,551,750 if the underwriters fully exercise their overallotment option), after deducting the payment of underwriting discounts and commissions of $1,875,000 (or approximately $2,156,250 if the underwriters fully exercise their overallotment option) and estimated offering expenses of approximately $292,000 payable by us.

We intend to use all of the net proceeds from this offering to redeem a portion of our outstanding Preferred Stock and/or for general working capital purposes.

Source: OXLCM Prospectus.

In the first extreme case, when all the proceeds are used to redeem already outstanding preferred equity, nothing fundamentally changes for the fund. In the second extreme, when all proceeds go for new asset acquisition, then the asset coverage ratio will drop to approximately 244%, leading Oxford Lane closer to the 200% regulatory required asset coverage minimum.

If we fail to maintain an asset coverage ratio of at least 200% (as described in this prospectus supplement), we will redeem a portion of the outstanding 8.25% Series 2031 Term Preferred Shares in an amount at least equal to the lesser of (1) the minimum number of shares of 8.25% Series 2031 Term Preferred Shares necessary to cause us to meet our required asset coverage ratio and (2) the maximum number of 8.25% Series 2031 Term Preferred Shares that we can redeem out of cash legally available for such redemption.

...

In addition, our Articles Supplementary for the Term Preferred Shares prohibit us from declaring a common stock distribution if, at the time of declaration, our asset coverage ratio is not at least 200% after deducting the amount of such distribution.

Source: OXLCM Prospectus

Please keep in mind that the total investment value as of December 31, 2025, was $2.26 billion. If this trend of fast devaluation continues, it will bring the fund even closer to the mandatory 200% ACR requirement and all the above-mentioned consequences of potentially breaching it.

Dividend Coverage

Since April 2026, Oxford Lane will pay its common shareholders the already decreased dividend of $0.20 per share monthly. As of February 28, 2026, the Company had approximately 97.6 million shares of common stock issued and outstanding. We can calculate approximately $19.5 million in expected common shares distributions monthly. The already outstanding preferred shares have approximately $14.1 million in dividends per year, and OXLCM will increase this value by $5 million to a total of approximately $19.1 million. This means that after the 50% cut, the common shares' dividend cushion is still more than adequate, providing 100% coverage of the annual preferred dividends with a month's worth of the OXLC distribution.

Oxford Lane products comparison

Besides OXLCM, Oxford Lane has three more term preferreds and four baby bonds outstanding. Here are their metrics at this moment:

And here are the IPO credit spreads:

The previous fixed-income IPO of Oxford Lane was the baby bond OXLCG a year ago. All else being equal, it is normal for the preferred OXLCM to have a higher risk premium. However, 90 basis points is a bit too much. So, underwriters also reflect the fact that the fund has become a bit riskier issuer compared to a year ago.

Looking at the nominal yields of the securities, how they are priced, and knowing that Oxford Lane can currently issue preferred equity at 8.25% and debt possibly at 7.75%-8.00%, it doesn't look like the company has the initiative to refinance anything but OXLCI. For all the reasons described above, I currently find no appeal in investing in any of the Oxford Lane fixed-income products. However, if I HAD to pick one, it would be OXLCZ. When a company becomes too risky for our liking, the shortest-duration product often provides the best risk/reward ratio. The debt lies higher in the capital structure, so this is also in favor of the baby bond over the preferreds.

Sector Comparison

The charts below contain all baby bonds and term preferreds with maturity dates in the closed-end fund—CLO segment. The stocks are currently priced under or at par, so the comparison will be by the YTM metric:

And the relevant metrics:

OXLCM has the highest YTM in this group. This, however, reflects the problems we pointed out so far. Five years to maturity is way too much for an exposure to a junior fixed-income product of a company that loses 50% of its NAV in 12 months. As I explained, our hedging reaction is gone, so this is another reason not to reach for any of the products. I'd like to see some NAV consolidation and/or OXLC priced at a premium before even considering putting some money into the fund's fixed income.

Mandatory Redemption for Asset Coverage

If we fail to maintain an asset coverage ratio (as defined below) of at least 200% as of the close of business on any Business Day on which asset coverage is required to be calculated, and such failure is not cured by the close of business on the date that is 30 calendar days following such Business Day (referred to in this prospectus supplement as an Asset Coverage Cure Date), then we are required to redeem, within 90 calendar days of the Asset Coverage Cure Date, shares of Preferred Stock equal to the lesser of (1) the minimum number of shares of Preferred Stock that will result in our having an asset coverage ratio of at least 200% and (2) the maximum number of shares of Preferred Stock that can be redeemed out of funds legally available for such redemption. Also, at our sole discretion, we may redeem a number of shares of Preferred Stock (including shares of Preferred Stock required to be redeemed) that will result in our having an asset coverage ratio of up to and including 285%. The Preferred Stock to be redeemed may include, at our sole option, any number or proportion of the 8.25% Series 2031 Term Preferred Shares and other series of Preferred Stock.

Source: OXLCM Prospectus.

Conclusion

This is an informational article about the new term preferred stock OXLCM. With these articles, we want to pay attention to all new preferred stocks and baby bonds, and they are a good guide to what to expect from your income portfolio.

Oxford Lane is currently not in a good place. The two dividend cuts in the past twelve months could very well limit the NAV erosion, and although a good move, they created another problem for the management. Cutting the distribution of the common stock in combination with a reverse stock split made the “income seekers” discard their positions at large, pricing OXLC consistently for months at a discount. This closed another channel for adding to the NAV—issuing new shares at a premium. For us, the discount means no more hedging reaction at a premium. This, in combination with the still dropping NAV per share, makes the investment in OXLC's fixed income unappealing at the moment.

data.title: Strategy: Soon A Long, But Not Yet
data.description:
Introduction

Compared to the broader market (SPX), Saylor-led Strategy Inc. (MSTR) has performed poorly since the beginning of 2026.

My first coverage appeared on December 19, 2025, rating the stock a Sell. Since then, MSTR is down around 23%. During the sharp selloff, however, it briefly dropped to an overall decline of 36%.

In this piece, I am arguing why I believe that Strategy continues to be headed for further downside in the near term and briefly why that very downside is likely going to present a buying opportunity. Let's dive in.

Developments

My previous thesis on MSTR was the following:

In my view, MSTR today looks like a sell, but there are also arguments for a Hold, because:

1. Current losses were mostly due to de-correlation. If Bitcoin (BTC-USD) prices decline further, MSTR has that much further downside plus a downside rerating in NAV premium.

2. Bitcoin prices could be closing in on a local, potential multi-year top, according to cycle theory.

3. Their now lower mNAV and a broken cycle theory could lead to a rebound from here.

From that point on, Bitcoin prices have continued to decline, having dropped around 25%.

Indeed, we have also seen Bitcoin have a slight rebound from around the time of my article until early January. It gained nearly 13%, before getting rejected at the 50-week moving average, a level that has offered strong support multiple times during the 2024/2025 bull market, and getting sent lower, forming a lower low and establishing a downtrend on the higher time frames, arguably starting the bear market, often referred to as "crypto winter".

Having topped out in Q4 2025, the post-halving year, is also consistent with every previous cycle. No top indicators have been swept. Nearly all of that is due to the fact that price did not appreciate as much as at previous cycles' highs, even in proportion. In my opinion, however, this can be largely attributed to the fact that the liquidity environment was completely different than especially during the last cycle. Interest rates remain elevated, and the absence of quantitative easing has made it hard for risk assets to perform. This is also portrayed in the performance of most altcoins, which did not reach new all-time highs.

It therefore seems fair to say that my thesis is playing out so far. It is not done doing so, however, as Bitcoin usually corrects by more than 70%. Peak-to-trough, we are now at just -52%. On the monthly chart, price is about to close a red candle with a large wick to the upside, potentially signaling continued downward pressure.

Other than cycle theory and technical indicators, there are also fundamental backdrops to enable the above to work in the first place. The most prevalent is the war in Iran. Bitcoin fell below $69k on renewed Iran-related stress, then bounced back above $71k when the U.S. strike timing was delayed, and has since slipped back toward the mid-$60k area. Most recently, the US is sending more ground troops there. Continued engagement and an ongoing closure of the Strait of Hormuz could pressure the world economy and prompt the Fed to hike rates instead of lowering them, which can act like poison on risk assets.

One of the most important concepts of MSTR is its mNAV. It reflects how euphoric or pessimistic investors are about the stock's future (and thereby mostly the price development of Bitcoin). At the moment, based on diluted shares outstanding, MSTR trades at an mNAV of just 0.94. Usually, a level of 1 is seen as fairly valued. Since the peak in late 2024 at 3, this measure used as a valuation metric for the stock has already compressed significantly. In my view, however, it could still go lower, as during 2022, it reached values of 0.5-0.7. A prerequisite for that is lower Bitcoin prices that would deplete investor sentiment. The combination of both of these instances can lead to significant further downside for Bitcoin from here. Two scenarios I could see happening and their effect on the stock price are:

  • BTC $50k, mNAV 0.7 = decline of 45%
  • BTC $40k, mNAV 0.5 = decline of 68%

Strategy continues to relentlessly acquire Bitcoin off the market, despite price and sentiment dropping. In March alone, Strategy bought 17,994 BTC for about $1.28B in the week ended March 8, then added another 22,337 BTC for roughly $1.57B in the week ended March 15, and still purchased a further 1,031 BTC for $76.6M in the week ended March 22, taking total holdings to 762,099 BTC. This is true even as prices dropped below their break-even price of $75k. To me, this proves that $75k is not some type of magical number that, once the price reaches it, will be a large catalyst for Strategy in any direction.

That is notable because one of the most common bear arguments was that Strategy would crack once Bitcoin fell materially below its cost basis. So far, that has not happened. As of March 22, the company’s average purchase price had climbed to $75,694 per Bitcoin, while Bitcoin now trades near $66,396. Despite that gap, Strategy did not sell.

The reason the balance sheet has held up better than many expected is that Strategy now has a more deliberate liquidity buffer. Management says it had a $2.25B reserve as of both December 31 and February 13, intended to cover preferred dividends and debt interest, while cash and cash equivalents stood at $2.30B at year-end. None of its outstanding convertible notes were convertible at holders’ option during the quarter ending March 31, 2026, either, which reduces near-term pressure.

Technicals from Strategy stock itself remain bearish, but that is not of concern, as buying during fear usually does not entail pretty charts or calm uptrends.

The future looks bullish for Strategy. If they fall further from here, I would view that as a potentially excellent entry point because the long-term setup for Bitcoin still looks structurally intact. Bitcoin’s issuance remains fixed and predictable, with total supply capped at 21 million, while US liquidity is again expanding, as M2 rose from about $21.6 trillion in February 2025 to about $22.7 trillion in February 2026. At the same time, the Fed still has the policy rate at 3.75%, but its March projections cluster future rates below that level and around roughly 3% over the longer run, which supports the case that the medium- to long-run rate path is lower. Meanwhile, the fiscal backdrop remains increasingly fragile, with CBO projecting a $1.9 trillion 2026 deficit and debt held by the public rising to 120% of GDP by 2036. Strategy stock specifically would offer exposure to a large and still growing Bitcoin treasury. So if Bitcoin resumes its long-term uptrend after a deeper drawdown, Strategy should benefit both from the appreciation of the underlying BTC and from renewed valuation expansion in mNAV, making the stock a leveraged expression of a macro and cycle recovery. Cycle theory may eventually break, but it has not yet. Therefore, the most prudent strategy moving forward is still positioning oneself in accordance with it.

Conclusion

In my view, Strategy is no longer an obviously overheated premium trade, but it is also not yet the kind of panic-priced asymmetric opportunity that tends to generate major alpha, as it was during the 2020 and 2022 lows. The company’s recent actions argue for balance-sheet durability, while the macro tape, Bitcoin volatility, and still-positive mNAV argue that a better entry may come later if the cycle weakens further. Once it reaches that, there will be more fear, uncertainty, and doubt. It will be mentally harder to invest in Strategy, since now the broader market does not yet seem pessimistic enough for it to generate significant alpha. For that we need more depressed valuations and sentiment, which I believe are coming later this year. That later point will present a buying opportunity, which I will then address separately in a bullish piece.

Rating: Maintain at Sell.

data.title: Vail Resorts: Poor Conditions And A Shakier Dividend (Downgrade)
data.description:
As we exit the calendar Q4 earnings season, companies have blamed all manner of external macro factors for poorer performance: a tough macroeconomy, weaker consumer spending, and we expect the war to play into earnings call commentaries in the upcoming quarter as well, especially for travel-related companies and those exposed to higher gas prices.

Vail Resorts, Inc. (MTN), the largest ski resort operator in the world, is blaming the weather. The company called out record-low snowfall in the Rockies, which led to sharp, unexpected declines in revenue. Shares of Vail are now down more than 20% in the past year. The key question for investors now is, do we hang on, especially given the rich ~7% dividend yield, or has Vail become more speculative?

I last wrote a “Buy” opinion on Vail in January, when the stock was trading in the high $130s. I'll cut to the chase here: Vail's fiscal Q2 (January quarter) performance and guidance cut was hugely disappointing, and I'm now quite nervous to retain my position and bank on a near-term solve. Recall that Vail is undergoing massive capex plans to transform and modernize its resorts this year, and improvements to guest throughput and profitability will take time to materialize. At the same time, the stock market today is rife with bargain-buying opportunities (especially in the software sector), making Vail less attractive. I'm cutting my rating on this stock to “Neutral.”

Guidance cut and revenue declines across all categories

Let's kick off the discussion with the elephant in the room: Vail's sharply disappointing Q2 update. I had anchored my previous “Buy” rating on Vail based on the company's prior Q1 season pass sales update, in which it said pass sales units had fallen 2%, but dollars had increased 3% y/y (due to the Epic Pass's continued trend of annual price increases). I had been expecting modest revenue growth this year.

Instead, in Q2 (the January quarter), Vail delivered $1.01 billion in mountain revenue (from all activities outside of lodging and real estate), down 4.8% y/y.

Total net revenue, inclusive of lodging and real estate, meanwhile, clocked in at $1.08 billion (-4.7% y/y) and missed Wall Street's expectations of $1.11 billion (-2.6% y/y) by a two-point margin.

Again, we had anchored to the expectation that lift revenue would increase on a y/y basis, given the fact that the company's previous season-to-date Epic Pass sales had seen cumulative dollar growth. Instead, visitation floundered, with total skier visits down 12.5% y/y and impacting lift ticket (non-pass) revenue. At the same time, the company's important ancillary sources of revenue also fell, with ski school revenue declining 9.3% y/y and in-resort dining and retail also falling nearly 7% y/y as well.

The company blamed the quarter entirely on the weather while still noting that Epic Pass price increases still drove cumulative dollar growth for the program. Per CEO Rob Katz's remarks on the Q2 earnings call:

Our second quarter was significantly impacted by these unprecedented weather challenges in the Rockies, which weighed on visitation and overall performance. In addition to Rocky snowfall through February being at historic lows, it's also been the warmest winter to date on record for Colorado. For example, February was 9 degrees warmer than average in the Rockies, which has dramatically impacted our ability to open terrain. This season saw the latest opening of the back bowls at Vail Mountain and Imperial Lift at Breckenridge, and only 70% to 80% of acres opened through the end of February at our resorts in Colorado and Utah. The Rockies are the largest driver of resort EBITDA for the company, and as such, the poor weather had an outsized negative impact on our results this year [...]

For our broader array of passes, we announced 3% to 4% price increases for Epic and Epic Local passes before taxes. Combined with our new 20% discount for young adults, price changes we made to Epic Day passes and other unlimited regional products, this results in an approximately 3% to 4% blended price increase before the impact of any mix changes. Additionally, this year, we are passing through the sales and lift tax the company pays to local communities on multi-resort passes in the same way we do for lift tickets and all of our other mountain products. The tax rate for most of our passes is approximately 3%.

Still, the difficult thing here is that with the core ski season now behind us (Q2 is by far Vail's biggest and most important quarter), we now have to wait until next year for Vail to prove it can recover, which is a tall ask.

Meanwhile, for the full FY26, Vail cut its guidance for resort-reported EBITDA (which is basically profitability in the mountain and lodging segments, excluding any gain/loss from real estate transactions) to $745-$775 million, the midpoint of which is a sharp 13% reduction from a previous $842-$898 million guidance.

Vail's dividend may be more difficult to maintain

The other core reality that we need to address is the fact that many investors hold Vail for its rich dividend. For now, the company has maintained the same $2.22 quarterly payout that it has paid out since the beginning of 2024, which at today's share prices translates to a ~7.0% annual yield.

That's not a bad reward for holding the stock, and it's this rich dividend plus the promise of positive pass sales and potential operational transformation that keep me from being overly bearish on Vail. But the question is, can this dividend be sustained?

Vail doesn't guide specifically to cash flow, but we can surmise the company's cash generation potential based on history. In FY25, Vail achieved $554.9 million in operating cash flow and total EBITDA of $862.8 million. This implies 64% conversion from EBITDA into cash flow.

If the same trends play out this year, Vail's $760 million midpoint resort EBITDA guidance would translate to $486 million in operating cash flow, assuming a similar conversion as last year.

As shown in the chart above, the company has reaffirmed its plan to invest $234-$239 million in capex this year, the bulk of which is directed toward modernizing its existing resorts. This would leave only ~$254 million in free cash flow at the midpoint.

Meanwhile, there are 35.78 million fully diluted shares outstanding as of the end of Q2. On a full-year basis, assuming no further share repurchases, Vail would have $317.7 million in dividend payout obligations (at the current $2.22 quarterly rate, or $8.88 per share per year). This now positions Vail's payout ratio at 125%.

Needless to say, this is unsustainable. We also don't see Vail tapping into its debt to be able to fund its dividend, as the company just exited Q2 at a 3.1x leverage ratio:

In short, we think that Vail's decision to maintain its dividend is a bit brazen, and we're nervous about the company eventually having to pause or cut down its dividend (as it did during COVID-19), which may affect sentiment further.

Key takeaways

In my view, Vail is no longer a comfortable long position. While I think the ~7% dividend offers great incentive to stay patient in this company, we think the recent sharp 13% cut to full-year adjusted EBITDA expectations, alongside poor sales results during the critical winter season, will make it very difficult for Vail to maintain its current payout. In my view, it's best to start trimming this position.

data.title: Commodities: Iran Hits UAE And Bahrain Aluminum Plants
data.description:
By Ewa Manthey, Commodities Strategist and Warren Patterson, Head of Commodities Strategy

Energy – Oil jumps on Iran war escalation

Oil prices jumped at the start of the week as the Middle East conflict intensified and Iran‑backed Houthi militants entered the war. Brent crude surged as much as 3.7% to $116.75/bbl, while WTI briefly moved back above $100/bbl, as fighting extended into a fifth week.

Yemen‑based Houthis launched ballistic missiles at Israel over the weekend, following US‑Israeli strikes on Iranian nuclear facilities, raising fears of a broader regional escalation. The group has previously disrupted Red Sea shipping, forcing vessels to reroute and heightening concerns over energy supply chains.

Risks were further amplified by the deployment of an additional 3,500 US troops to the region and renewed rhetoric from US President Donald Trump, who said he wants to “take the oil in Iran” and could seize the export hub of Kharg Island.

There remains little sign of imminent peace talks.

Tight physical market conditions are also evident in the forward curve, with Brent’s prompt spread in deep backwardation. The front‑month contract traded at a premium of more than $7/bbl on Monday, compared with a largely flat structure before the conflict, underscoring acute near‑term supply concerns.

Emergency stock releases are starting to trickle through but remain limited relative to the scale of the disruption. France has released around 580kb of oil products under the IEA’s coordinated emergency programme, roughly 4% of its 14.5mb commitment, while the overall IEA release could add around 400mb to global supply, led by a 172mb contribution from the US Strategic Petroleum Reserve.

Positioning data shows speculative interest remains elevated despite some trimming. Speculators reduced their net long in ICE Brent by 21,579 lots to 407,125 lots as of last Tuesday, driven largely by a reduction in gross longs, while positioning changes in NYMEX WTI were modest. Ongoing geopolitical uncertainty and supply risks have continued to underpin risk‑on positioning in the oil market so far this year.

European natural gas prices also moved sharply higher, rising as much as 3.4%, as the escalation in the Middle East reinforced concerns over LNG availability ahead of the summer storage refill season. Prolonged disruptions risk slowing injections into European storage and intensifying competition for spot LNG cargoes at a time when global supply flexibility is limited. Adding to the pressure, a weeks‑long outage at a major Australian LNG facility due to storm damage has removed additional supply from the global market, amplifying Europe’s exposure to external shocks as it heads into the injection season.

Metals – Aluminium jumps 6% after fresh Middle East attacks

Aluminium supply risks in the Middle East intensified over the weekend after Iranian attacks hit two major regional producers. Emirates Global Aluminium (EGA) said it sustained significant damage, while Aluminium Bahrain (Alba) is assessing the extent of disruption to its operations. Aluminium climbed as much as 6% to $3,492/t in early trading on the LME.

EGA confirmed several employees were injured, but has not yet clarified whether operations at its smelters in Abu Dhabi and Dubai have been suspended. EGA is the largest aluminium producer in the UAE and one of the biggest globally.

The latest escalation comes on top of already tightening supply conditions across the Gulf. Recent curtailments at Alba and Qatalum have already affected around 560kt of annual capacity, leaving close to 8-9% of regional supply at risk. With the Middle East accounting for roughly 9% of global aluminium production, any prolonged outages would further tighten the market, especially given the region’s limited raw material inventories and dependence on uninterrupted shipping through the Strait of Hormuz.

We remain constructive on aluminium prices. The latest attacks increase the probability of a prolonged disruption scenario, where supply losses could persist even if geopolitical tensions ease, reinforcing upside risks to prices.

Positioning across metals remains mixed. CFTC data shows speculators reduced net long positions in COMEX copper by 10,860 lots for a 13th consecutive week to 35,802 lots, the least bullish positioning since September 2025. Managed money net longs in COMEX gold also fell, down 13,145 lots to 92,775 lots, driven largely by a reduction in gross longs, while silver saw a modest increase in speculative net longs, supported by fresh long entries.

Agriculture – US raises biofuel quota

The Trump administration has raised US biofuel blending mandates, requiring refiners to blend a record 25.82bn gallons of biofuels into gasoline and diesel this year – nearly 8% above last June’s guidance. The higher quota reflects elevated gasoline prices linked to the Iran conflict, reviving interest in biofuels as a cost‑containment tool. While smaller refiners warn of higher compliance costs, the policy is expected to support both the energy and agriculture sectors, providing income relief for US farmers amid weak crop prices and rising input costs.

In Brazil, the Agriculture Ministry has advised farmers to delay fertiliser purchases after prices spiked on Middle East tensions. With winter crop planting largely complete, near‑term fertiliser demand has softened, giving farmers more flexibility in procurement, while highlighting Brazil’s continued reliance on imported fertilisers.

Brazil’s sugar market remains in focus as production winds down. UNICA data shows cane crushing in Centre‑South Brazil fell 29.7% year-on-year in the first half of March, with sugar output down 88.6% YoY as the season nears its end. Despite weaker near‑term output, cumulative sugar production is slightly higher YoY, while speculative positioning has turned less bearish, with net shorts in No. 11 raw sugar falling for a third consecutive week. The shift reflects aggressive short‑covering and tighter supply expectations, partly driven by increased diversion of cane to ethanol.

CFTC data shows mixed positioning across US grains. Net speculative longs in CBOT soybeans fell for a second consecutive week on fading optimism around US‑China trade talks, while corn saw a sharp increase in bullish bets driven by short‑covering. In wheat, net shorts continued to unwind, leaving positioning at its least bearish level since mid‑2022, supported by new long positions and concerns that higher fertiliser costs could influence planting decisions later in the year.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more.

Original Post

data.title: Kimbell Royalty Partners: Average 2026 Quarterly Distribution Projected At $0.47 Per Unit
data.description:
Kimbell Royalty Partners (KRP) expects its 2026 production to be roughly similar to its 2025 production. Around 32% of its production is oil, of which approximately 20% is hedged for 2026. Thus, it benefits a reasonable amount from higher oil prices, and I project its average 2026 quarterly distribution to be $0.47 per unit now at the current strip. This is compared to its $0.36 per unit quarterly distribution during 2H 2025.

I have increased my estimate of Kimbell's value from $16.50 per unit to $17.50 per unit in my base case scenario using the current 2026 strip ($84 WTI oil) and then $70 WTI oil and $3.75 NYMEX natural gas after that time. An upside scenario with $95 WTI oil in 2026 and $75 WTI oil after 2026 would increase its estimated value further to around $19.00 per unit.

2026 Outlook

The midpoint of Kimbell's 2026 production guidance is for 25,500 BOEPD (32% oil, 20% NGLs, and 48% natural gas) in average production. This is fairly close to its 2025 production, which ended up at 25,760 BOEPD (33% oil).

The current strip for 2026 involves roughly $84 WTI oil and $3.85 NYMEX natural gas. At those commodity prices, Kimbell is projected to generate $379 million in revenues after hedges.

Kimbell has roughly 20% of its 2026 oil and natural gas production hedged, resulting in an estimate of negative $11 million in 2026 hedge value. Around $10 million of that negative amount is due to its oil hedges.

Kimbell is thus projected to generate $270 million in 2026 distributable cash flow, or approximately $2.50 per unit.

Kimbell's 75% payout ratio would translate into an average quarterly distribution of close to $0.47 per unit during 2026.

With that 75% payout ratio, Kimbell would have roughly $68 million to put towards debt reduction and unit repurchases. It recently initiated a $100 million common unit repurchase program.

Kimbell has also mentioned the potential to redeem some of its remaining $162.5 million in Series A Preferred Units during the second half of 2026, although the cash interest rate for its credit facility is currently slightly higher than the 6% preferred distribution rate. That redemption would reduce the potential dilution from the conversion of the preferred units (with a $15.07 conversion price).

Upside Scenario

In an upside scenario with unchanged natural gas prices, but an average of $95 WTI oil in 2026, Kimbell would generate approximately $300 million in 2026 distributable cash flow (or around $2.78 per unit). This would result in an average quarterly distribution of around $0.54 per unit at a 75% payout ratio.

This scenario would involve an average of $103 WTI oil from April to December 2026, compared to around $88 to $89 WTI oil during that period at current strip prices.

Estimated Valuation

I previously estimated Kimbell's value at $16.50 per unit, but I am increasing that to $17.50 per unit in a scenario involving current strip prices and then long-term (after 2026) $70 WTI oil and $3.75 NYMEX natural gas.

This increase in its estimated value is mostly due to the improved distributable cash flow projections due to higher oil prices. I have also assumed that Kimbell can realize around $3.25 for its natural gas (before hedges) starting in 2027, with some improvements to its natural gas differentials compared to 2026.

Kimbell's estimated value would improve further to around $19.00 per unit in an upside scenario that involved an average of $95 WTI oil in 2026 followed by long-term (after 2026) $75 WTI oil. That upside scenario assumes a $5 increase to long-term oil prices due to higher ongoing geopolitical risk.

Conclusion

Kimbell Royalty Partners benefits a decent amount from higher oil prices and should be able to deliver a quarterly distribution of around $0.47 per unit during 2026 at current strip prices (including around $88 to $89 WTI oil during the last three quarters of 2026). This projected distribution would increase to around $0.54 per unit with a roughly $14 increase (compared to strip) in oil prices during the last three quarters of the year. These distributions would be approximately 30% and 50% higher, respectively, than Kimbell's average quarterly distribution during 2H 2025.

I now estimate Kimbell's value at approximately $17.50 per unit in a scenario with the current 2026 strip and then $70 WTI oil and $3.75 NYMEX natural gas after that time. This increases to $19 per unit at a $95 WTI oil average during 2026, followed by $75 WTI oil and $3.75 NYMEX natural gas after that time. This assumes around a 9% cash distribution yield with a 75% payout ratio (or a 12% yield based on total cash available for distribution).

data.title: USA: Discount Opens Up, Creating A 'Buy' Opportunity (Upgrade)
data.description:
Written by Nick Ackerman, co-produced by Stanford Chemist

The Liberty All-Star Equity Fund (USA) has seen significant pressure recently, with volatility in equities starting to rise. Over the last year, the fund has also seen a significant widening in its discount. As a closed-end fund, the market price can trade significantly differently from the actual underlying net asset value per share.

This creates discounts/premiums that can be taken advantage of, often providing opportunities. Just a year ago, USA was still flirting with a premium to NAV. That wasn't all that uncommon for the fund since around 2021, when it began to fairly regularly trade at a premium or near parity with its NAV. Given this near 10% discount now opening up and greater overall pressure in equities, it makes USA an interesting choice once again.

USA Basics

  • 1-Year Z-score: -1.09
  • Discount/Premium: -9.65%
  • Distribution Yield: 12.81%
  • Expense Ratio: 0.91%
  • Leverage: N/A
  • Managed Assets: $2.06 billion
  • Structure: Perpetual

USA's investment objective is to "seek total investment return, comprised of long-term capital appreciation and current income. It seeks its investment objective through investment primarily in a diversified portfolio of equity securities."

When constructing the portfolio, the fund is split between five different managers, each with a value- and growth-oriented tilt. While the fund is listed as 60% value, we'll see below that the portfolio's largest allocation is to technology. It also holds many of the Magnificent 7 names near the top.

Performance - Discount Gets Wide

The last time we discussed USA specifically in a write-up was all the way back in mid-2023. At that time, we were also discussing Gabelli Equity Trust (GAB). Both of these funds are pretty vanilla equity funds, which don't do anything too exciting. That made them a perfect pair to consider for a swap trade, which ended up seeing USA outperforming significantly at the time.

Since then, GAB has taken the lead by a wide margin. They had correlated quite closely until the last couple of months, during which time GAB's total NAV return also started to outperform significantly.

The differences in their portfolio are the reason for this, as USA holds a heavier tech allocation to NVIDIA (NVDA), Alphabet (GOOGL)(GOOG), Microsoft (MSFT), Amazon (AMZN), and Meta Platforms (META). It isn't overly dominated by tech, given the 60% sleeve of value-oriented managers, but it is the fund's largest sector allocation. With these sorts of names starting to take a breather after several years of solid runs, that has pressured GAB.

On a YTD basis, none of these names are positive, with MSFT down quite sharply. In fact, MSFT is in bear market territory, with its decline from a 52-week high of nearly 30% now.

That can be compared with GAB, which is an even more value-oriented portfolio. The largest allocation is financial services for GAB, with no Mag 7 exposure in its top ten at all.

With that said, USA's total share price returns have lagged materially further compared to GAB and its own total NAV returns. With that occurring, that tells us that the fund has moved to a discount. In this case, the discount has become quite significant relative to its short-term and long-term average levels.

This often represents an opportunity to start considering a position when this occurs. Combine that with the broader equity market, which started to get more volatile, and that can also indicate an even better time, as the Nasdaq has entered pullback territory.

When we look back further, a wide discount isn't all that uncommon. Between 2008 and around 2017, it frequently traded at a 10%+ discount. So that is always the caveat that just because a fund's discount is wide now, it doesn't mean that it can't stay that way or get even wider. That also includes the possibility that the overall market can drop further, and the drop can be quite significant.

Additionally, when USA is compared to the Lipper Large-Cap Core Average, it has regularly underperformed on a total NAV and share price return basis—certainly not something to boast about. All these managers, and apparently none of them are adding any value, but that can be why buying more tactically at relatively larger historical discounts can still make sense. In fact, I'd argue it is the only time to consider investing in this fund based on the track record.

The performance comparison below is straight from the fund's website, so at least they aren't trying to hide anything.

Managed 10% Distribution Policy

The argument against investing in this fund from some investors is likely that the distribution rate is high. High-yield investors will enjoy that the distribution policy is targeting a 10% rate based on the NAV per year. This is broken down as paying out 2.5% of the NAV each quarter to investors.

Of course, this means they are paying this whether it is being earned or not. As NAV declines, the payout will also decline and vice versa. Thanks to the strong equity returns since around the Global Financial Crisis, we have seen a rising payout trend.

Given the equity focus, it is not surprising to see that the fund's payout will be funded nearly entirely from capital gains from the underlying portfolio. Net investment income tends to be fairly minimal with an all equity portfolio that holds a high allocation to the mega-cap technology stocks. They offer rather low dividend yields, which are mostly consumed by the fund's expenses—thus, limited NII.

On a per-share basis, NII came out to $0.04 in 2025 against the $0.67 paid out. Though, as we can see above, in the last two years, the fund was able to realize significant capital gains. Not quite enough to cover the entire payout for 2025, which would have otherwise seen the net assets decrease.

That said, the fund's reinvestment plan meant they would provide reinvesting through new shares being issued. That helped to push the net assets positive for the year based on the new shares being issued.

During the years ended December 31, 2025 and December 31, 2024, distributions in the amounts of $83,486,419 and $84,411,874, respectively, were paid in newly issued shares valued at market value or net asset value, but not less than 95% of market value. Such distributions resulted in the issuance of 12,651,928 and of 12,311,548 shares, respectively.

However, it should be noted that this occurs whether the fund is at a discount or a premium. USA is part of a smaller group of CEFs that still issue new shares even when the fund is at a discount. Most other funds have it in their policy that it will only be done when the fund is at a premium. If it is done at a premium, it is accretive to NAV; when done at a discount, it is dilutive.

In terms of the tax character of the distributions, we see the bulk being long-term capital gains.

Even better, the relatively minimal portion classified as ordinary income was considered 100% qualified in 2025. That makes it generally tax-friendly and could be held in a taxable account.

USA's Portfolio

As we noted above, the largest holdings in the fund are allocated toward the Magnificent 7 names. That helps to push the fund's highest sector allocation toward the information technology sector. However, this isn't as significant as it has become in the S&P 500 Index these days, with tech now pushing over 32%.

In looking at the full holdings, it comes out to 136, so it is otherwise fairly diversified. As we can also see, the weightings of each position start to drop fairly quickly after the first handful of names.

Given that the value-oriented managers manage 60% of the fund, we can see financials and healthcare as the second and third largest allocations. Though their representation among the top ten is relatively minimal compared to the tech names.

With an average portfolio turnover rate of 23% for the last five years, the fund is fairly active. At the same time, several of the same Mag 7 names appeared as top holdings in our last write-up. As a reminder, that goes back to mid-2023—pushing over two and a half years ago at this point.

Conclusion

USA has seen pressure recently on its share price and NAV, but its share price has taken an even heavier hit over the last year. A year ago, the fund was flirting with a premium, as it has done for a number of years now. However, we've seen the fund drop to a near double-digit discount. That can be quite discouraging for investors who owned this a year ago, as the downside move has been exacerbated. On the other hand, for investors today, it makes it a much more interesting choice to consider.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Argan Remains Bullish As Underlying Power Demand Is Still Very Healthy
data.description:
Investment summary

My previous investment thought on Argan, Inc. (AGX) was a buy rating because the backlog visibility was solid, the business still has a very strong balance sheet to fund this growth, and I didn't think the Q3 revenue decline was structural. Firstly, Q4 2026 showed that the Q3 revenue decline was mostly timing, as growth returned across all three segments and profitability improved sharply. More importantly, AGX still has a very healthy $2.9 billion backlog that is fully committed by customers and should convert into revenue over the next 3+ years. With demand for new gas-fired power plants still strong and margin mix improving, I continue to see AGX as a buy.

4Q26 results update

I don't think there are any complaints to be seen in this quarter. Revenue saw $262.1 million, up 12.7% y/y, with the growth coming from all three business segments. Power revenue saw $204 million, industrial revenue saw $53 million, and Teledata saw revenue of $5 million. The higher gas plant pricings are also starting to show up in margins. At the gross profit line, consolidated gross profit came in at $65.6 million (25% margin, up from 20.5% last year); and by segments, gross margins in Q4 2026 were 29% for Power, 11% for Industrial, and 14.2% for Teledata. Below gross profit, there was a slight inch up in SG&A expense as a percentage of revenue, from 6.4% last year to 6.8%, which was $17.9 million, but overall adj. EBITDA grew to $56 million, and EBITDA margin jumped to 21.4% from 16.9%.

Growth setup is attractive

An important point this quarter is that AGX has basically confirmed that the softer Q3 revenue was just timing. As you can see, Q4 2026 returned to double-digit revenue growth, and it was driven by organic means: the recently awarded Power projects started to ramp up while Trumbull continued to contribute. More importantly, AGX's backlog remains very healthy at $2.9 billion (was $3 billion in Q3 2026). Note that this backlog is fully committed by customers and should convert into revenue over the next 3+ years. My opinion is that gas power plants will remain the most attractive power source for the time being, and demand will not ease in the near-term.

As a reminder, the demand for power is not just driven by AI-related investments. Yes, data center build-out is one of the fastest (if not the fastest) drivers (AI data center electricity use has been growing at ~12% annually since 2017, much faster than overall electricity demand), but there are other demand areas like EV charging, US manufacturing onshoring, aging power assets that need replacement, etc. as key drivers. Renewables are not a viable option today because they are unstable, and the years of underinvestment has left the grid constrained (not effective in dealing with fluctuating power generation from renewables). To put things into perspective, electricity demand is expected to grow by 25% by 2030 and 78% by 2050, while 104 GW of power plant retirements are expected by 2030. In other words, this is not just about higher demand. It is higher demand coming online when the system is also losing a bunch of older supply.

Management's comments during the call were also very bullish. They said they continue to see strong demand for new gas-fired facilities and expect natural gas projects to remain a substantial part of the backlog in the near and mid-term. There is also one more point here that I thought was important. While being a "solver" for the current EPC problem is positive for AGX's business, it is not the only bottleneck. There are also constraints in the supply chain, such as gas turbine availability, labor, and interconnection approvals. Management noted that these conditions are improving, so it is a positive takeaway.

We should also start to see more apparent margin expansion moving forward. As more of the newer, higher-value gas power plant projects start flowing through the P&L, I think margin expansion should become more visible. Management already said AGX is moving into a more gas-heavy backlog mix, which is a major data point since these projects carry better pricing and margin. I would remind readers that this is still a project-based business, so intra-quarter volatility (for margins) is expected. But the mix shift itself does point to a stronger margin base over time.

Valuation

AGX continues to execute extremely well. Previously, I anchored my valuation against consensus FY28 adj. EBITDA estimates ($227 million), making the basic assumption that AGX should easily meet this estimate given its history of beating estimates and also the strong backlog. Its performance in the latest quarter sends a very clear signal that underlying demand remains robust, and the healthy backlog has also convinced consensus that adj. EBITDA could be higher (as you can see in the graph above, consensus has revised FY28 adj. EBITDA up to $244 million). The more bullish estimate calls for adj. EBITDA of $317 million in FY28, for reference.

On valuation, the EPC players have seen valuation go up as they are the bottlenecks to gas power plant build in the US. AGX now trades at 35x forward EBITDA, a steep premium to Quanta Services (PWR), which trades at 25x. I like to think that AGX could potentially continue trading at this multiple, given the solid backlog and apparent strong underlying demand. At 35x, that would translate to an EV of $8.5 billion and a market cap of ~$9.4 billion vs. the current market cap of $7.9 billion.

Risk

The main risk is timing. At the end of the day, project starts are controlled by owners, not AGX, so backlog conversion may get pushed back if they believe demand is not as strong as I thought. Another point is on margins. Q4 margin expansion was helped by early Trumbull completion, which means the coming quarters may see margin dip before it recovers as the higher-margin projects flow in. Lastly, labor remains tight, which could be a problem if labor inflation goes up by more than expected.

Conclusion

I am reiterating my buy rating for AGX. Q4 confirmed that underlying demand remains strong, and backlog quality is still solid. AGX should also start to show a better margin profile as more higher-value gas projects flow through the P&L.

data.title: Dividend Growth Bi-Weekly Chat 03/30/2026
data.description:
Welcome to the forum for Dividend Growth Investing discussion on Seeking Alpha. A new article is posted every two weeks as a space for sharing of ideas, discussing concepts, and digging deeper on DGI. All previous blogs are listed in chronological succession on the main chat page.

As promised and with your valued feedback, we are publishing a new version of the article with some changes to make it more engaging. The structure of the article will now include a response from one of you in the community regarding your thoughts on DGI.

If you’d like to share your DGI thoughts with us in future editions, you can email us at moderation@seekingalpha.com and let us know. We’ll be looking at continuing to do this moving forward.

For a reminder, you can find our moderation guidelines for this space in our profile. And please share your thoughts below to continue the discussion and learning on DGI.

More on Dividend Growth Investing:

How Our 7-Funds Portfolio Earns 7.8% And Potentially Beats The Market

The portfolio has delivered a trailing 12-month yield on cost of 9.42% and an annualized return (CAGR) of 14.67%, outperforming the S&P500 with 20% lower volatility. This strategy is designed for retirees seeking high, consistent income and reduced volatility without sacrificing long-term growth potential.

The Biggest Risk In BDCs, And How I Mitigate It As A Bull

The average debt to equity for private BDCs is 0.94x, which offers sizeable access to additional liquidity (debt) to meet the redemptions in case through organic loan repayments it is not possible. Similarly, based on recent examples, it is clear that there is a "NAV-aligned" market to sell parts of the portfolio (without fire-sale discounts). In the worst case, the alternative asset managers themselves could step in with the necessary firepower.

5 Lessons That Completely Reshaped How I Approach Dividend Investing

On the surface, dividend investing looks very simple: simply buy a diversified portfolio of stocks that pay attractive dividends and then sit back, relax, and let the dividends flow. However, the reality is that dividend investing is not that simple, as there are many hidden landmines that can quickly destroy your passive income stream and your wealth if you are not careful.

The Greatest Risk To Your Portfolio Is You

Volatility is picking up in the market, and we're seeing the market reacting to global events, which by definition are unpredictable. Will the market crash? Absolutely. It is an inevitable reality that is the equivalent of asking whether it will snow in Alaska. Will it happen in 2026? That is the harder question to answer.

These Aren't 'Just Picks' - I'd Build My Retirement Portfolio Around These 2 Stocks

If long-term rates remain elevated, ELS's total return will likely be mostly driven by its dividend and mid-single-digit per-share AFFO growth. It won't mean it's dead capital, given its valuation, yet it would likely result in single-digit annual returns.

Seeking Alpha's Disclosure

Past performance does not guarantee future results. Content is provided for information purposes only and does not constitute investing advice. Any views or opinions expressed do not reflect those of Seeking Alpha as a whole. Seeking Alpha does not take account of your objectives or financial situation and does not offer any personalized investment advice. Seeking Alpha is not a licensed securities dealer, broker or US investment adviser or investment bank.

data.title: My Top 6 Rebound Picks
data.description:
Introduction

What a wild couple of weeks the market has seen since the war in the Middle East reaccelerated, and it’s all happened after major fears over AI, jobs, and CapEx spending in 2026. As a result, the S&P 500 (SPX) has dropped about 7% YTD, while the Nasdaq Composite (IXIC) is down almost 10%. My own risky portfolio, the one I usually discuss here on Seeking Alpha, has dropped about 10% over this timeframe, which is not surprising given my heavy focus on the tech sector and AI.

I know how painful it can be for investors, and especially for those who are just getting started. I started this specific portfolio in April 2025, one year ago, adding money gradually, slowly at first, and investing most closely to the mid-end of 2025. Even though I've left about 20% of my cash untouched, it's hard to view this drop as a buying opportunity while my portfolio is in the red.

At the same time, I am not in this position for the first time. As a Ukrainian living in Israel who began investing (never did it before) only one year before the war in Ukraine began and the market went into a 2022-2023 pullback, taking my newly created portfolio into 2 years of "losses", I can tell you with confidence - today I feel like a kid in a candy shop.

In 2023, my portfolio was down by about 50% since its inception. I wasn't panicking, as I liked my "bets" nonetheless, and I had other things and problems to worry about. Long story short, later, in the span of one year, that exact portfolio multiplied by 4, without me touching it, and then, after I finally decided to come back and manage it actively, it shot up some more. Overall, with the drop in 2026, my old portfolio is up by 110%, while the S&P 500 is up by 63%. I am actually very surprised I was lucky enough to beat the S&P by a margin like this, and I say "lucky" because I was.

I just started investing a little over 5 years ago, and I had little clue what I was doing. If I were not depressed over the war in my home country throughout 2022-2023, I am not sure I wouldn't get the money out of this account and call it a day. Well, I hope I would not, as I had Palantir (PLTR) there, and I found the business to be fascinating, but still, if you ask me, not my genius approach has made this return, but just the fact that I simply LEFT THE MONEY ALONE. If I added some more over time when the market was down, then I would say the approach was smart.

So today, if you are unable to make yourself buy the "dip", my best advice would be to close the brokerage app/website and come back in a few quarters, or better, a couple of years. If you are not just getting started, and I know my 5-year experience is still a baby compared to many of the Seeking Alpha readers, then let's talk about the stocks I am picking up myself right now. These are my favorite picks for a market rebound, with the thought that the war may continue, and the market may go down way stronger than it did until today. Those will be the stocks that, in my opinion, will come out strong from almost any kind of event or environment and will make great returns in the next 2-5 years.

Micron

I have to start this list with Micron (MU), as this is one of a handful of stocks I rate as a Strong Buy on Seeking Alpha and my YouTube channel. Not only do I have a full position in my newer portfolio from last year, but I am also adding on this recent post-earnings drop.

Since my last coverage on March 18th, the stock is down by 16%, and this is one of the easiest choices I have to make at the moment. I am still in major plus on my overall position, and as I said before, my position was what I considered "full" when I started with Micron. Today, it is way larger, but this is a risky portfolio, so I will be adding more on this dip.

Most of the pullback happened due to the overall market volatility based on fears, and Micron looked "too good to be true" in comparison to the rest of the tech sector, which was struggling or flat.

Another reason for the drop was Alphabet's (GOOG) research on a "new" algorithm, which could allow for taking down storage requirements for training of AI models. It was a sensitive time for news like this, and the stock reacted accordingly. However, the drop in the stock price and even loud headlines from Google don't necessarily mean the demand for memory is going to stop. There are a couple of major reasons for this, and the first and most important one is that the algorithm isn't really new. It was worked on for quite some time, and in general, every time we make AI 2x more efficient, developers find a way to make the models 4x more complex. Besides, even if the data is compressed, the speed at which it needs to be accessed remains one of the major problems, and it means the demand for High Bandwidth Memory (HBM) will still be intact. Add to this the shortage of regular DRAMs for all of the regular devices and all of the contracts that sit in the backlog, and you'll easily see why Micron's rally has all the means to continue.

Uber

Uber (UBER) is another stock that I am personally really keen on, and I hold a large position myself. I started buying at a level of a little over $60, and I was still adding for $90. The stock is down once again to the high $60s, and I will be adding to my already big position because I am convinced Uber is deeply misunderstood by the market.

Historically, Uber and Tesla (TSLA) have often moved in opposite directions - whenever Tesla stock rallies, Uber goes down because the market buys into the narrative that Tesla’s autonomous vehicles and robotaxis will eventually leave Uber with no business and no market. I’ve been explaining why this is an incorrect argument before the market started discussing it so heavily. Back in April of last year, I wrote that driverless cars are actually a massive tailwind for Uber, and Citi, in its recent research, also said that the primary facilitator of AV trips by 2029 will be Uber.

I just rated Tesla as a strong sell in an article that is focused on the robotaxi segment. I explained in all detail why I don’t believe Tesla has the technology or a viable path to monetization to dominate (or, in fact, to be just an operational company in) this space. Uber is strategically positioning itself at the center of the AV transition through partnerships with companies like Amazon (AMZN), NVIDIA (NVDA), automakers like Lucid (LCID), and Rivian (RIVN). Uber is building a flexible platform that can integrate whatever technology is better off, and if it's not going to be back, Uber will still have its large base of clients.

Beyond just cars, Uber has Serve (SERV) for food delivery. I believe automated delivery in large cities will be the real, immediate life-changer, unlike robotaxis, which I am fairly skeptical about.

For these reasons, I think Uber is a misunderstood and safe bet. If you’re bullish on AVs, Uber is the best way to play the infrastructure of that future. If you’re bearish and think the AV hype is overblown, Uber is still the best pick because it remains the leader in mobility, and if the AV narrative disappears, Uber stock will recover nicely. The fact that they have a capital-light business model also helps me make this decision.

4 Out of MAG 7

I’ve decided not to give separate titles to each of these 4 picks, on purpose, because they have similar traits right now. They’ve been majorly sold off on AI fears and the general market drop I explained at the beginning of this article. Some have specific news or issues fueling the drop, some are down more, some less, but overall, I view all of them as great buying opportunities because of their business models, underlying metrics, and the fact that this market panic is overdone.

People are constantly talking about the CapEx AI bubble, comparing it to the dot-com era. I’ve explained why this argument is entirely incorrect in my article about Nvidia, and the logic is the same for these companies. If you want to say that today looks like that period, you have to compare the metrics, and when you do that, it's clear that right now, while it may be volatile, it's nothing like dot-com. Back then, valuations were sky-high with no profits or revenue to back them up. Margins weren't growing because the only fundamentals that were improving were made up. This time, we see a huge demand, and we see a real breakthrough in technology and in our computing capabilities.

Among the Mag 7, Amazon is my first pick. I rated it a strong buy until the massive CapEx spending they promised this year. After I saw it, I started doubting if they actually have the money to cover this CapEx without additional problems, and I think they are taking an unnecessary risk, so I re-rated it to a Buy. But even so, with the stock down 14% year-to-date, I think this is a great entry point.

I think that the companies that have their own clouds will be able to use that capacity themselves if the demand cools off at some point, and Amazon is among them.

That brings me to Alphabet, which has an incredibly strong cloud business. I’ve written a full article on them as well, and after this sell-off, it’s definitely time to buy more for me. Their backlog is enormous, and in the case of Google, I think their CapEx is entirely justified.

Then there’s Microsoft, which dropped the most, down 26% year-to-date. The story is similar, but added to by the weird fears that AI will take jobs from software. I’ve explained before how disconnected that is in a few articles of mine, one about software, another about Microsoft directly. The market can’t be afraid of massive CapEx (which means the AI will prove to be not as huge as everyone hopes now) and simultaneously afraid that SaaS companies won't make money because AI will be huge and will take people's jobs. Those two fears don't work together, so this drop is overdone. I’ve initiated a position myself, and I think Microsoft is a great buying opportunity.

Finally, there’s Meta (META), a company I haven't actually covered on SA before. Just two days ago, the stock plunged 12% due to lawsuits regarding age concerns and child safety. I don't actually rely on that when I look at Meta. I look at them because they are, and will continue to be, one of the best digital advertisers in the world, and I am very bullish on this industry in general. They have the data and the knowledge to create something very powerful on their platforms, or even beyond them.

I find the underage argument less interesting, as I actually hope that kids under 16 will be blocked from all social media, like Instagram, TikTok, Facebook, all of it. There is an argument that if the kids will not use it now, they will also not use it as adults, which ruins the long-term prospects for Meta. But this is wrong in my opinion. There are plenty of things like alcohol, spicy foods, relationships, etc., that people don't do as kids but start when they grow up. Plus, young users aren't really important, as you are almost unable to monetize them. You want the 25-to-30-plus audience who earns and spends money.

Conclusion

I feel very comfortable holding these stocks long-term. Micron looks like it will be the shortest holdout of them for now. I will be following it closely and trimming at some point, potentially closing this position to wait for the cycle to turn. I don’t know what will happen short-term with the war or if oil prices will push the US into a recession, but as a long-term investor, I am not worried about what happens with my money in these positions. If we do hit a recession, the financial sector will struggle most, but the tech sector and these specific companies will be the ones to bounce back the fastest when it all calms down.

These are not the only stocks I am buying right now, but these are the stocks I am planning on having a significant position in. Other picks of mine include defense, which I was buying before the beginning of the war; some software, which sold off; and other tech companies of different sizes. I have small healthcare, retail, and airline exposure. You can find an analysis of almost every stock I, personally, hold on my page.

data.title: Meituan: A Messy Quarter, But Underlying Trends Are Positive
data.description:
Summary

I upgraded Meituan (MPNGF)(MPNGY) to a buy rating in my previous update, as I believed the revenue weakness was not as bad as it seemed. I also thought the other new initiatives were contributing nicely, and importantly, MPNGF continues to win share, and subsidies used have trended lower. Overall, I stay bullish on MPNGF because the business looks better than the headline Q4 loss suggests. Core Local Commerce is still under pressure, but the main point here is that competition now looks more rational, food delivery economics are improving sequentially, and the moat should get stronger with more membership program adoption.

Earnings results update

In the latest quarter (Q4 2025) reported last week, MPNGF grew revenue by 4.1% y/y, delivering total revenue of RMB92.1 billion. Within it, core local commerce revenue was RMB64.8 billion, down 1.1% y/y, while new initiatives revenue was RMB27.3 billion, up 18.9% y/y. For New Initiatives, Keeta and grocery retail were the main growth drivers, with Keeta expanding into four new markets, while Xiaoxiang Supermarket ramped up city expansion and warehouse build-out. While revenue growth was positive, gross profit fell to RMB24.1 billion (down from RMB33.4 billion in Q4 2024). Combined with selling and marketing expenses surging to 34.4% of revenue, from 19.6% last year, and other operating costs growing, as a result, MPNGF posted an operating loss of RMB16.1 billion in Q4 2025. On an adj. EBITDA basis, loss was RMB 14 billion.

Membership Program Moat

I urge readers to focus not on the reported revenue line but on the quality of the user base. To me, the most important aspect for MPNGF's business is whether its moat is strengthening, as that is what determines the long-term growth profile. The encouraging thing here is that management made it clear that MPNGF is still winning in terms of frequency, cross-category usage, and consumer mindshare. Looking across the entire FY2025, the facts are that platform GTV and transaction volume both saw double-digit growth, while new transacting users, transaction frequency, and ARPU all reached new highs. This is not by luck; it is driven by a structural driver: MPNGF has fully upgraded its membership program and expanded it across nearly all categories. We know that the value proposition is resonating, as management noted the richer member benefits improved user engagement and transaction frequency enough to push a notable share of users into higher membership tiers.

That is a lot more important than it may seem. My sense is that a lot of investors still look at MPNGF mainly as a food delivery platform that can only win through scale and logistics. I don't think that is true. MPNGF has evolved to become more of a local services ecosystem, where one service pulls demand into another. To make that work, the membership program is the one that binds all the services together so that users are not just dealing with a group of separate products. Essentially, the membership program gives users a reason to stay inside the platform, try more categories (since they are already subscribing to it), and, over time, build habits. The ideal outcome is that this turns occasional users into repeat users, and repeat users into heavier platform users (aka high-value members). Having more high-value members means that MPNGF faces less competitive pressure since these members are unlikely to churn (they have already formed a habit and are committing to spending on MPNGF's platform).

Management said the core user base kept growing and retention improved vs. Q3, which is bullish because it suggests the platform is still expanding its broader user base. Over time, that creates a larger pool of users that can be upgraded into higher-value members.

Food delivery

The biggest bullish takeaway in this past quarter is that food delivery now looks less like an open-ended price war and more like a market slowly moving back toward rational competition. The regulatory stance is also now very clear, which should reignite confidence in investors that this irrational subsidy-driven competition will ease, which should bring back the "margin recovery" story. If we look at MPNGF's numbers, that certainly appears to be the case. In Q4, management focused on quality growth and further pulled back resources from low-AOV and low-quality orders. That resulted in a sequential recovery in food delivery AOV. While it is also fair to point out that reported revenue still fell in Core Local Commerce because incentives remained high and food delivery AOV was still down sharply y/y, the underlying direction got better. Core local commerce operating loss improved meaningfully sequentially from RMB14 billion to RMB10 billion (largely due to better food delivery losses).

The near-term setup looks better as well. Management said it is on track to see a more meaningful sequential improvement in food delivery per-order loss in Q1 2026 vs. Q4 2025. I would not call this a full recovery yet. But still, it does suggest that Q4 may be the trough of this cycle, and once Q1 2026 shows further improvements, the market should start to price in more margin improvements given the line of sight to the end of this price war."

Valuation

MPNGF's valuation has trended even lower than when I previously wrote about it, falling from 10x two-year forward EBITDA to ~8.7x as I write this update. I think the main cause is because the market was worried about a permanent "price war," which would mean earnings growth will not be material for the near term. I don't believe that is going to be the case. MPNGF is actually actively pulling back from going after low-quality orders, and the user quality remains strong. In fact, MPNGF is still capturing demand from the high-value consumer base.

Also, despite all these competitive headwinds, this is still a business that grew revenue by high single digits, so I don't think it is hard to imagine growth accelerating once the "price war" headwind eases (which is what the regulator wants). Consensus expects growth to accelerate back to low-to-mid-teens, which is not a high bar, but the important line is that adj. EBITDA is still expected to surge significantly from negative to RMB60.6 billion (~almost 3x FY23 levels, albeit this is still a downward revision from my previous update).

Therefore, my view on MPNGF's ability to show robust adj. EBITDA growth has not changed, and with that, I also don't think the current valuation is justified. I'd say MPNGF should trade closer to where Uber (UBER) is, given both of them are dominant players in their region and MPNGF has a stronger adj. EBITDA growth outlook. I need to note that the recent market sell-down has dragged down UBER's valuation, so it is fair that MPNGF is unlikely to trade as high as I thought previously.

Applying UBER's current two-year forward EBITDA multiple to MPNGF implies RMB60.6 billion * 10x two-year forward adj. EBITDA = RMB606 billion in enterprise value. MPNGF has about RMB 80 billion in net cash, so market cap is ~RMB 686 billion, or a share price of RMB 122 (USD 17.65).

Investment Risk

Core local commerce revenue was still down y/y even though transaction count and GTV held up better, because incentives remained high and food delivery AOV was still under pressure. If incentives continue to stay high, MPNGF could keep showing healthy user activity, but margin may take longer to recover, and that would weigh on the stock sentiment. Specifically, the stock may stay cheap for longer even if the underlying platform metrics look better.

Conclusion

My buy view stays intact. Q4 2025 was still a messy quarter in reported profit, but the direction of change looks positive. User engagement is getting better with the membership program, the food delivery price war appears to be easing, and management is pulling back from low-quality orders, which should help margins recover over time.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
